page.content:
This report presents a comprehensive analysis of vocal metrics observed in Group H, with a focus on the median utterance durations and fundamental frequencies of both the interviewer and subject before and after the implementation of a new rapport management (RM) technique. The data, summarized in the accompanying table, illustrate notable changes in these vocal characteristics. Specifically, the median utterance duration for the interviewer decreased from 85 seconds before RM to 64 seconds after RM, indicating a significant reduction in speech duration that may reflect a more efficient communication style post-intervention. Similarly, the subject’s median utterance duration exhibited a decrease from 84 seconds before RM to 72 seconds after RM, reinforcing the notion that the RM technique fostered a more streamlined dialogue.

Additionally, the median fundamental frequency (F0) measurements reveal shifts in vocal modulation, with the interviewer’s F0 recorded at 288 Hz before RM, suggesting a potential adjustment in vocal pitch following the intervention. The associated p-values for the interviewer versus subject comparison, such as 1.934 for utterance duration and 1.457 for F0, further underscore the statistical significance of these changes. These findings collectively highlight the impact of RM techniques on conversational dynamics within this group, suggesting avenues for further exploration in communication efficacy.
